metadata.title: VW to pixels converter — CSS viewport width to px calculator
metadata.description: Convert CSS vw units to pixels at any viewport width. See fluid typography clamp() values — free, no registration, works in browser.

hero.title: Convert vw to px – fluid layout at any viewport width
hero.description: 1vw changes with every screen size. Enter your vw value and viewport width to see the exact pixel output — essential for clamp() fluid typography.

software.name: VW to pixels converter
software.description: Convert CSS viewport width (vw) units to pixels based on screen width. Essential for responsive web design and layout calculations.
featureList: Real-time vw to px calculation at any viewport width
Shows result for both desktop and mobile breakpoints
Copy result to clipboard in one click
Works entirely in browser — no server calls
Reverse conversion: px back to vw

howTo.name: How to use the vw to px converter

steps.name: Set viewport width
steps.text: Adjust the viewport width in pixels.

contentBlocks.title: When do you need to convert vw to pixels?
contentBlocks.html:
The CSS vw unit equals 1% of the viewport width. On a 1920px screen: 1vw = 19.2px, 50vw = 960px, 100vw = 1920px. The actual pixel value changes with screen size.

This converter helps you calculate the pixel value of vw units at specific viewport widths, useful for responsive design debugging and documentation.

vw is commonly used for fluid typography (font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem)), full-width sections, and responsive spacing.

All calculations run locally in your browser — nothing is sent to any server.

items.title: 1. Enter a value
items.description: Type a number in the input field. You can use a period or comma as the decimal separator.

items.title: 2. Read the result
items.description: The conversion result appears instantly in the field next to it – no clicking required.

items.title: 3. Copy or reverse
items.description: Click Copy result or use the Reverse button to convert in the opposite direction.

items.title: Full-width hero sections and overlays
items.description: Sections set to width: 100vw span the entire viewport including scrollbar width — which can cause horizontal overflow on Windows. Use this converter to confirm that your hero section at 100vw matches your expected container max-width, and to decide whether width: 100% is a safer alternative.

items.title: Fluid typography with clamp()
items.description: The CSS clamp() function creates text that scales between a minimum and maximum size. font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem) sets a floor at 16px, a ceiling at 32px, and scales fluidly in between. This converter tells you exactly what 2.5vw equals at 375px (9.4px) vs 1280px (32px), so you can calibrate clamp() arguments with confidence.

items.title: Container max-width and viewport comparison
items.description: Most layouts cap content at max-w-7xl (1280px) or max-w-6xl (1152px). At a 1440px viewport, the content fills 88.9vw — the remaining vw is padding. Knowing the vw-to-px relationship helps you design responsive padding that looks balanced across phone (375px), tablet (768px), and widescreen (1920px).

items.title: CSS debugging across device breakpoints
items.description: When an element is set to 45vw and looks fine on desktop but too wide on mobile, you need to know the pixel values at each breakpoint. At 1920px, 45vw = 864px. At 375px (iPhone SE), 45vw = 168.75px. This tool makes cross-device comparison instant without needing DevTools.

items.title: Complete privacy
items.description: All calculations run locally in your browser. No data is sent to any server.

items.title: Real-time results
items.description: The result updates instantly as you type – no need to click any button.

items.title: Bidirectional conversion
items.description: The Reverse button lets you convert in both directions with one click.

items.title: Reference table
items.description: A ready-made table with commonly converted values and contextual descriptions.

contentBlocks.title: How does vw to px conversion work?
contentBlocks.html:
The formula is: px = vw × viewport_width / 100.

At 1920px viewport: 1vw = 19.2px. At 1280px: 1vw = 12.8px. At 375px (iPhone): 1vw = 3.75px.

Related units: vh = 1% viewport height, vmin = 1% of smaller dimension, vmax = 1% of larger dimension.

contentBlocks.title: Practical tips
contentBlocks.html:
• 100vw includes scrollbar width. Use width: 100% for content width without scrollbar.
  • Fluid typography: font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem) limits minimum and maximum size.
  • Common viewports: 375px (iPhone), 768px (iPad), 1280px (laptop), 1920px (desktop).
  • vw is recalculated on window resize. CSS calc() works with vw: width: calc(100vw - 2rem).

items.question: What is 1vw in pixels?
items.answer: 1vw = 1% of the viewport width. At 1920px screen: 1vw = 19.2px. At 1280px (laptop): 1vw = 12.8px. At 768px (iPad): 1vw = 7.68px. At 375px (iPhone SE): 1vw = 3.75px. The value changes dynamically as the browser window resizes.

items.question: Does 100vw include the scrollbar?
items.answer: Yes. 100vw includes the scrollbar width, typically 15–17px on Windows Chrome. This can cause horizontal overflow when combined with full-width elements. Use width: 100% for content containers, or use 100dvw (dynamic viewport width) in modern browsers to exclude the scrollbar.

items.question: What is the difference between vw and % width?
items.answer: vw is always relative to the browser viewport (window width), regardless of nesting. % width is relative to the parent element's width. Inside a 600px container, 50% = 300px but 50vw = 50% of the full viewport, which could be 640px on a 1280px screen.

items.question: How do I use vw in a clamp() function?
items.answer: font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem) creates fluid typography with a minimum of 16px, a maximum of 32px, and a mid-point of 2.5vw. At 640px viewport, 2.5vw = 16px (the minimum kicks in). At 1280px, 2.5vw = 32px (the maximum kicks in). Between those points, the font scales proportionally.

items.question: What is 100dvw and how is it different from 100vw?
items.answer: 100dvw is dynamic viewport width, a modern CSS unit that accounts for mobile browser chrome (address bar) collapsing. On mobile Safari, 100vw includes the address bar height area, while 100dvh/dvw adjusts as the bar shows or hides. Most browsers support dvw since 2023.

items.question: Can I use vw for padding and margins?
items.answer: Yes, though use it sparingly. margin-inline: 5vw creates gutters that scale with viewport width — on a 1920px screen that is 96px, on a 375px phone it is 18.75px. This technique works well for hero sections, but can produce too-tight layouts on small screens if not bounded with a min() or max().

items.question: How does vw relate to CSS container queries?
items.answer: Container queries use cqw (container query width) instead of vw. cqw is relative to the nearest container, not the viewport. On a page where a component lives in a 600px sidebar, 50cqw = 300px while 50vw would still equal 50% of the full page width.

items.question: Are calculations done locally?
items.answer: Yes. All calculations run in your browser. No data is sent to any server.
